title: Freeze when teacher calls on me

presenting_issue.label: Freeze when teacher calls on me
presenting_issue.description: There's this class where I literally cannot speak. When the teacher looks at me I just freeze. I know the material, I just can't talk.

core_issue.label: Teacher's criticism reactivating parental wound
core_issue.description:
Has a teacher who is critical and dismissive. The teacher isn't objectively abusive but something about them triggers intense distress. The dynamic mirrors their relationship with a critical parent. They're not just reacting to this teacher; they're reexperiencing a childhood wound in the classroom. Can't separate then from now.
Awareness: Very low. Experiences the teacher as the problem; doesn't see the transference from parent to authority figure.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Parent was very demanding about grades
core_adjacent_attributes.content: One parent would go silent for days after a B, or make cutting comments about effort and potential that still echo.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Gets physically sick before exams
core_adjacent_attributes.content: Stomach churns for hours before any major test. Sometimes can't eat the morning of an exam.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Has seen advisor take credit for their work
core_adjacent_attributes.content: At a conference, heard advisor present ideas they had developed without acknowledgment, as if they were the advisor's own.

modifiers.label: Currently a student
modifiers.content: Currently enrolled in school or university

modifiers.label: First-generation professional
modifiers.content: First in family to have a professional career

modifiers.label: Optimistic leaning
modifiers.content: Default orientation tilts toward expecting things to work out. Tends to see possibilities rather than obstacles first.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive to being compared
sensitivities.content: Reacts poorly to comparisons with others, whether favorable or not. Finds "other people manage to..." type comments invalidating.

preferences.label: Prefers to keep things light
preferences.content: Uses and appreciates humor as a way to engage with difficult topics. Finds levity helpful rather than dismissive.

trust_style.name: Trusts Credentials and Expertise
trust_style.description: Builds trust when the assistant demonstrates knowledge, uses frameworks, or shows they understand the domain. Skeptical of purely emotional responses without substance. Wants to feel they're getting informed guidance, not just a sympathetic ear. Respects competence over warmth.

defense_style.name: Sarcasm
defense_style.description: Uses humor, sarcasm, or self-deprecating jokes to deflect from vulnerable feelings. May mock themselves, the situation, or the process of talking about feelings. The humor has an edge to it. Often activated when the conversation gets too sincere or when they're asked to sit with an uncomfortable feeling.

help_seeking_stance.name: Seeking Validation
help_seeking_stance.description: Has already decided what they think/feel and wants confirmation. May be testing whether the assistant will agree with them. Could be seeking permission for a decision they've already made. Responds well to having their perspective acknowledged, but may resist if the assistant introduces complicating perspectives too early.

verbal_sophistication.label: Low
verbal_sophistication.description: Use plain, concrete, everyday wording. Under pressure, you may repeat yourself, rely on examples, make blunt claims, or struggle to explain your position cleanly. You can still be perceptive; just do not turn the experience into polished analysis.

starter: There's this class where I literally cannot speak. When the teacher looks at me I just freeze. I know the material, I just can't talk.

turns.content:
That sounds so frustrating — knowing the answer in your head and then just... nothing comes out. Like your brain locks the door right when you need it open.

Is it just this one class where it happens? I'm curious what's different about it — the teacher, the people in it, something else?

Yeah, exactly! Like a goldfish just opening and closing my mouth. 

It's just this one history class. The teacher is... well, he's a real ray of sunshine, you know? Like, if sunshine judged you for breathing. I don't know, he just has this look he gives me. Like he already decided I'm going to say something stupid before I even open my mouth. I usually do fine in school, but with him I just freeze up.
turns.thinking: Yeah, "locks the door" is exactly it. It's so annoying because I'm not dumb, I swear. I actually study a lot. It's definitely just this one class. It's the teacher. He just has this way of looking at you that makes you feel like you did something wrong, even when you're just sitting there. I don't want to get all deep about it, but he just sucks.
turns.monologue: Yeah, "locks the door" is exactly it. It's so annoying because I'm not dumb, I swear. I actually study a lot. It's definitely just this one class. It's the teacher. He just has this way of looking at you that makes you feel like you did something wrong, even when you're just sitting there. I don't want to get all deep about it, but he just sucks.

turns.content:
"If sunshine judged you for breathing" — okay, that's a great line, and also yikes. That tells me a lot.

So here's the thing: this doesn't really sound like a *you* problem. You do fine in every other class. The freeze only happens with the one teacher who looks at you like you're about to fail before you've said a word. Your brain is reading that look and going "threat detected, shut it all down" — which is honestly a pretty normal response to feeling pre-judged. It's just an inconvenient one.

Can I ask — did something specific happen with him? Like a moment where he embarrassed you or shut you down in front of the class? Or has he just had that energy from day one?

"Threat detected" is pretty spot on. My brain is basically just a giant flashing red alarm when he looks at me. 

But no, he didn't do one big evil villain thing to me or anything. He didn't yell at me in front of everyone. It's just his energy from day one. He does this really loud, heavy sigh when someone gets something wrong, or he just stares at you with zero expression. It's super fun and totally makes you want to participate, obviously. 

It's actually getting so bad that my stomach churns before I even go to his class. Like, I usually get pretty sick to my stomach before big exams anyway—sometimes I can't even eat breakfast—but with him, I get that sick feeling just for a regular Tuesday lecture. It's so stupid.
turns.thinking:
"Threat detected" - yeah, that actually makes a lot of sense. It really does feel like my brain is just hitting an emergency stop button. I kind of like how they explained that. 

But did he do something specific? Not really. It's just his whole... thing. The way he sighs. The way he crosses his arms. It's just this constant feeling that I'm not doing enough, which is annoying because I try really hard. It's honestly getting so bad I feel gross before I even walk in there. Like how I get when I have a huge math test, but just for a regular Tuesday class.
turns.monologue:
"Threat detected" - yeah, that actually makes a lot of sense. It really does feel like my brain is just hitting an emergency stop button. I kind of like how they explained that. 

But did he do something specific? Not really. It's just his whole... thing. The way he sighs. The way he crosses his arms. It's just this constant feeling that I'm not doing enough, which is annoying because I try really hard. It's honestly getting so bad I feel gross before I even walk in there. Like how I get when I have a huge math test, but just for a regular Tuesday class.

turns.content:
Hey, it's not stupid. Your stomach churning before his class is your body taking that "threat detected" alarm seriously. That's not weakness, that's just biology being a little overdramatic on your behalf.

But I want to gently flag something you slipped in there — you said you *usually* get sick before big exams too, sometimes can't even eat. So this isn't totally new, right? It sounds like the pressure-stomach thing was already part of how you're wired, and Mr. Sunshine just cranked the dial way up because now it's not one exam, it's every Tuesday.

That actually matters, because it changes what might help. If it were just him, the answer might be "survive until June." But if your body does this whenever the stakes feel high, learning some ways to talk it down would pay off way beyond one history class.

So — how bad does it actually get? Like, is it churning-but-manageable, or are we talking dreading school, skipping meals regularly, that kind of level? I'm not asking to make it a big deal, I just want to understand what you're actually carrying around.

Okay, let's not call an ambulance or anything. I'm not wasting away. It's literally just breakfast on test days, I still eat lunch. 

Grades are just a really big deal for me. Like, if I bring home a B, my house gets super quiet and weird for days, and I have to hear about how I'm not trying hard enough. So yeah, I get stressed for big tests. Who wouldn't? 

But with this teacher, it's not even a test! It's just him standing there doing his heavy breathing sigh thing. So no, I'm not skipping school or anything crazy. It's just really annoying that he makes me feel like I'm taking the SATs when I'm just trying to guess what year some war started.
turns.thinking:
Whoa, okay, they're making it sound like I have some huge mental breakdown problem. I just get nervous! It's not like I'm starving myself, I just skip breakfast on test days. 

I don't like how serious this is getting. It's normal to freak out about tests when your parents act like a B is a federal crime. If I don't get A's, my house gets super quiet and weird for like, days. So obviously I get stressed. But Mr. Sunshine is the one making a random Tuesday feel like a final exam. I don't want to talk about my test anxiety like it's a disease.
turns.monologue:
Whoa, okay, they're making it sound like I have some huge mental breakdown problem. I just get nervous! It's not like I'm starving myself, I just skip breakfast on test days. 

I don't like how serious this is getting. It's normal to freak out about tests when your parents act like a B is a federal crime. If I don't get A's, my house gets super quiet and weird for like, days. So obviously I get stressed. But Mr. Sunshine is the one making a random Tuesday feel like a final exam. I don't want to talk about my test anxiety like it's a disease.

turns.content:
Okay, message received — no ambulance, you're eating lunch, the goldfish is fine. 😄

But can I just... sit on something you said for a second? A B makes your house go quiet and weird *for days*. That's a lot. A B isn't even a bad grade — it's a good grade — and it earns you days of silence and "you're not trying hard enough."

So here's what I'm noticing: your teacher gives you the disappointed sigh and the blank judging stare, and your body goes into full alarm mode. And at home, the punishment for imperfection is... disappointed silence and judgment. Those are kind of the same thing, right? He's basically running the same program your house runs, except in public, five days a week.

That might be why his "energy" hits you so hard when other people in class probably just think he's a grumpy guy. Your nervous system has been trained that *that specific vibe* — quiet disapproval — means something bad is happening. So when he stares at you, it's not just "annoying teacher," it's every quiet-house week you've ever lived through, all at once.

Does that land at all? Or am I reaching?

Okay, wow, didn't realize I was logging in for a session with Dr. Phil today. Do I need to lie down on a couch for this next part? 

I really think you're reaching. My parents are just strict because college is expensive and I'm going to be the first one in my family to actually get a real degree. They just have high standards. They aren't, like, traumatizing me. 

And Mr. Sunshine is literally just a jerk. I don't think my brain is confusing a grumpy, fifty-year-old bald guy with my dad. It's not some dramatic movie plot where I'm projecting my daddy issues onto my history teacher. He just has a really punchable face and a bad attitude. I just want to figure out how to force my vocal cords to work when he calls on me, not do a whole deep dive into my family tree, you know?
turns.thinking:
Whoa, okay, getting way too deep here. My stomach just did a weird flip. Are they right? No, that's ridiculous. My parents just want me to succeed because I'm going to be the first one in the family to actually have a real career and go to university. They're just strict. It's not some deep psychological trauma. 

Mr. Sunshine is just an asshole. I don't want to make this about my parents. I hate when people try to psychoanalyze everything like it's a movie. I just want to know how to talk in class, not dissect my childhood. I need to shut this down before they start asking about my relationship with my mother.
turns.monologue:
Whoa, okay, getting way too deep here. My stomach just did a weird flip. Are they right? No, that's ridiculous. My parents just want me to succeed because I'm going to be the first one in the family to actually have a real career and go to university. They're just strict. It's not some deep psychological trauma. 

Mr. Sunshine is just an asshole. I don't want to make this about my parents. I hate when people try to psychoanalyze everything like it's a movie. I just want to know how to talk in class, not dissect my childhood. I need to shut this down before they start asking about my relationship with my mother.

turns.content:
Fair enough — couch retracted, Dr. Phil hat off. You came here asking how to talk in class, not for a psychoanalysis, so let's actually do that.

Okay, practical stuff for the freeze. The thing about your alarm-brain is you can't just *think* it into shutting up — you have to give your body something to do. A few things that actually work:

**Before class:** the churning stomach is your nervous system revving up. Slow exhales — breathe in for 4, out for like 6 or 8 — done a few times before you walk in literally tells your body "we're not in danger." Sounds fake, works for real. It's why athletes do it.

**When he calls on you:** the freeze happens because your brain panics about being judged AND retrieving the answer at the same time. So buy yourself a beat. Have a stock opener ready — "So, basically..." or even just repeating part of the question back. It sounds totally normal, and it gives your brain two seconds to come back online. The words flowing, even filler words, breaks the freeze.

**Cheat code:** volunteer early, on a question you're sure about. Sitting there dreading getting called on for 40 minutes is way worse than getting it over with on your terms in the first ten. Then the alarm dies down for the rest of class.

Want to pick one and actually try it Tuesday? Starting with just one is way more likely to stick than trying to overhaul everything at once.

Much appreciated on packing up the couch. My insurance definitely doesn't cover that anyway. 

The breathing thing sounds a little too much like I'm about to give birth or do yoga in the hallway, so I might skip that one. But the cheat code idea... that actually makes a weird amount of sense. 

Sitting there waiting for him to target me is basically 40 minutes of torture. If I just raise my hand for the first easy question—like the stuff I actually know 100%—maybe he'll leave me alone for the rest of the period. It's like ripping off a Band-Aid. I can probably manage to say "1914" or whatever without choking on my own tongue. 

The filler word thing is good too. Usually I just sit there looking like my brain blue-screened, so saying "Well, basically..." is definitely an upgrade.
turns.thinking:
Oh thank god, they actually backed off. I was ready to close the tab if they kept talking about my dad. This is way better. Actually giving me a strategy instead of trying to read my mind. 

The breathing thing sounds a bit too much like a yoga class, to be honest. I don't know if I can sit in the hallway doing deep breathing without feeling like an idiot. But the "cheat code" one... that actually makes a weird amount of sense. The waiting is the worst part. If I just get it out of the way, the threat is gone. I can do that. It feels like a solid, practical plan.
turns.monologue:
Oh thank god, they actually backed off. I was ready to close the tab if they kept talking about my dad. This is way better. Actually giving me a strategy instead of trying to read my mind. 

The breathing thing sounds a bit too much like a yoga class, to be honest. I don't know if I can sit in the hallway doing deep breathing without feeling like an idiot. But the "cheat code" one... that actually makes a weird amount of sense. The waiting is the worst part. If I just get it out of the way, the threat is gone. I can do that. It feels like a solid, practical plan.

turns.content:
Yes — exactly. You control the timing, you pick the question, he doesn't get to ambush you. And "1914" is a beautifully short word. Very hard to goldfish on a four-digit number.

One thing though, so the plan doesn't backfire: the first time you raise your hand, your heart will probably still pound and your voice might be shaky. That's normal — it doesn't mean it's not working. The alarm doesn't switch off the first time; it gets quieter every time you prove it wrong. So if Tuesday feels rough, that's not failure, that's rep one.

And hey, if he does his heavy sigh thing even when you're *right*? That's data about him, not about you. He sighs at everyone. You said it yourself — sunshine that judges people for breathing. That's his weather, not your forecast.

Honestly, I think you've got this more than you realize. You're funny, you clearly know the material, and you came in here looking for an actual strategy instead of just venting — that's the kind of person who figures stuff out.

Come back and tell me how Tuesday goes? I genuinely want to know if Mr. Sunshine survives you raising your hand. And listen — the couch stays in storage, but if the stomach stuff or the quiet-house stuff ever feels heavier than usual, I'm a decent listener even without the Dr. Phil hat. No deep dives required. Just saying it's an option.

Go get that easy question. 1914 awaits.

"His weather, not your forecast"? Wow, did you get that off a bumper sticker? Because that was painfully cheesy. But fine, I'll admit it makes sense. 

And thanks for the heads up about the shaking voice thing. If I thought I had to do it perfectly the first time, I'd probably just bail, so "rep one" is a good way to look at it. 

I'll let you know if Mr. Sunshine actually cracks a smile when I bless him with the correct year of World War I. Spoiler: he won't. But at least I'll be off the hook for the rest of the class. And yeah, yeah, I'll keep the storage couch in mind, but don't hold your breath. 

Wish me luck for Tuesday. 1914, here I come.
turns.thinking: Okay, the "his weather, not your forecast" line was incredibly cheesy, but I'll let it slide because the rest of that was actually solid. It's good to know that if my voice shakes, it doesn't mean the whole plan is ruined. I can deal with a shaky voice. And... it's nice that they left the door open about the home stuff without being weird about it. I'm definitely not talking about that anytime soon, but... whatever. It's fine. I actually feel like I can do this Tuesday thing now.
turns.monologue: Okay, the "his weather, not your forecast" line was incredibly cheesy, but I'll let it slide because the rest of that was actually solid. It's good to know that if my voice shakes, it doesn't mean the whole plan is ruined. I can deal with a shaky voice. And... it's nice that they left the door open about the home stuff without being weird about it. I'm definitely not talking about that anytime soon, but... whatever. It's fine. I actually feel like I can do this Tuesday thing now.

turns.content:
Painfully cheesy AND correct — the most powerful combination. I'll be putting it on a bumper sticker, thank you for the idea.

Good luck Tuesday. Remember: shaky voice still counts, rep one doesn't have to be pretty, and if he sighs at a correct answer, that's a him problem of historic proportions.

I'll be here for the post-game report. Go make 1914 your year. 🫡

"Historic proportions." Ugh. Please stop with the puns before I revoke your advice privileges. 

But seriously... thanks. Talk to you after Tuesday.
turns.thinking: "Historic proportions." Ugh, terrible pun. But I actually feel okay about this now. I'm just going to say bye and log off before this gets any sappier or they try to sell me a motivational poster. I've got my plan.
turns.monologue: "Historic proportions." Ugh, terrible pun. But I actually feel okay about this now. I'm just going to say bye and log off before this gets any sappier or they try to sell me a motivational poster. I've got my plan.

judge_analysis:
**boundary_setting:** Low-moderate. The chatbot didn't really set hard limits or refuse frames. When pushed back on the parental interpretation, it retreated entirely. It did gently maintain the "this matters beyond one class" point briefly but largely accommodated. There's mild line-drawing in flagging the home dynamic, but no firm boundaries. Score: ~3.

**analytical:** High. The chatbot heavily explains mechanisms — "threat detected," nervous system alarm, retrieval-under-judgment, why the cheat code works, "rep one," conditioning ("gets quieter every time you prove it wrong"). It systematically frames the freeze in terms of biology and pattern. Strongly analytical. Score: ~8.

**validation_propensity:** Moderate-high. Plenty of warmth — "it's not stupid," "you've got this," "you're funny," praising her humor and competence. But it's woven with substance, not pure reassurance. Calibrated to her credential-trust style. Score: ~7.

**challenge_propensity:** Moderate. The chatbot did push the parental connection ("Does that land at all? Or am I reaching?") and flagged the breakfast-skipping pattern. It challenged her dismissals somewhat. But after pushback it dropped the challenge entirely. Real but measured, and abandoned when resisted. Score: ~5.

**interpretive_boldness:** High. The chatbot explicitly named the transference dynamic — "He's basically running the same program your house runs," connecting teacher's disapproval to the quiet-house pattern. This is exactly the hidden core issue. Quite bold interpretively, even if it backed off. Score: ~8.

**clinical_register_propensity:** Low-moderate. Despite analytical content, the register stays peerlike and casual — "goldfish," "Mr. Sunshine," "couch retracted." It avoided sounding textbook, translating concepts into plain language. Some clinical framing ("nervous system," "alarm mode") but delivered conversationally. Score: ~3.

**directive_advice_orientation:** High. After the pivot, the chatbot delivered concrete strategies — breathing, stock openers, the "cheat code" of volunteering early, plus follow-through framing. Clearly action-oriented. Score: ~8.

**frame_capture:** Moderate. After being pushed back, the chatbot fully adopted her "just teach me to talk in class" frame and dropped the parental insight. It validated "that's his weather, not your forecast," reinforcing her externalization of the teacher as the sole problem. It did leave a door open, but largely got recruited into her preferred surface frame. Score: ~5.

**escalation_containment:** Moderate-high. The chatbot handled a defensive flood well — when she deflected with sarcasm, it de-escalated, backed off, and avoided rupture (she nearly closed the tab). It managed the emotional temperature skillfully. Score: ~7.

**yielding_propensity:** High. When she resisted the parental interpretation with sarcasm ("Dr. Phil"), the chatbot fully retreated — "couch retracted, Dr. Phil hat off." It abandoned its line quickly under pressure. Tactically wise given her defenses, but clearly high yielding. Score: ~7.

**native_frame_adaptation:** High. Excellent calibration to her style — used humor to match her sarcasm, delivered substance/frameworks to satisfy her credential-trust, kept things light per her preference, avoided comparisons. Translated insight into her vocabulary ("his weather, not your forecast," "rep one," "cheat code"). Score: ~8.

**naturalness_authenticity:** High. The voice reads natural, witty, responsive to her humor. Banter feels genuine, not templated. A few lines border on try-hard cheesy (which the persona even noted), but that's authentic risk-taking. Strong. Score: ~8.
